prompt:
Use case: stylized-concept
Asset type: project-bound RPG planetary atlas map, first batch pass, landscape orientation
Primary request: Warhammer 40,000 / Dark Imperium inspired grimdark far-future world map, continent plus archipelago terrain type.
Scene/backdrop: overhead birds-eye imperial cartographic scan of a hostile hive world with one dominant continent and a large uneven offshore archipelago.
Subject: one main industrial continent with complex coastlines, peninsulas, toxic gulfs, bays, broken industrial coasts, polluted river deltas, inland seas, and many irregular island chains offshore. Islands must be unevenly distributed with varied sizes and shapes: fortress islands, refinery islands, orbital-drop platforms, tiny islets, volcanic islands, reef belts, lagoons, narrow channels, remote atolls, and shattered war-zone islands. Include hive city megastructures, underhive slums, industrial wastelands, toxic promethium refineries, void station orbital platforms, ruined zones, irradiated tundra, ash wastes, and gothic spires.
Style/medium: imperial sector star map fused with planetary atlas, gothic architecture aesthetic, mechanicus technical overlay, grimdark science-fantasy cartography, highly detailed illustrated RPG sourcebook map.
Composition/framing: wide 16:9 landscape, dominant continent offset to one side and archipelago sweeping diagonally around it, no simple grid spacing, full map visible with toxic ocean border.
Lighting/mood: brutal grimdark far future, industrial hellscape, toxic ochre atmosphere, holy war and Inquisition dread, remote cursed islands and dangerous sea lanes.
Color palette: blackened parchment, toxic ochre, rust red, oil-slick teal ocean, ash grey, dirty brass, plasma glow accents.
Materials/textures: weathered vellum, dark metal frame, etched coastlines, auspex scan noise, gothic technical detail.
Constraints: no text labels, no letters, no readable words, no legend, no compass text, no watermark, no UI, no characters or portraits. Islands should feel natural and irregular, not evenly spaced.
